section.title: Teaching Sound Doctrine
section.sectionRationale: Over against the false teachers, Titus is to teach what accords with sound doctrine. Paul works household by household, older men and women, young women and men, and slaves, showing what godliness looks like in each, then grounds the whole ethic in the great gospel statement of the grace of God that has appeared and will appear again. Instruction and gospel form one movement.
section.thesis: Sound doctrine produces beautiful lives across every station, and the engine of that transformation is the grace of God that has appeared in Christ to save and to train us, as we await the glorious appearing of our great God and Savior.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the household instructions that adorn the teaching about God our Savior; then the theological heart, the grace of God that has appeared, redeeming a people zealous for good deeds, with a closing charge to Titus to speak with authority.

units.label: Sound doctrine in the household
units.note: godliness for every age and station

verses.text: But as for you, speak the things that are consistent with {{hygiaino|sound}} doctrine.
Older men are to be temperate, dignified, {{sophron|self-controlled}}, and sound in faith, love, and perseverance.
Older women, likewise, are to be reverent in their behavior, not slanderers or addicted to much wine, but teachers of good.
In this way they can train the young women to love their husbands and children,
to be self-controlled, pure, managers of their households, kind, and submissive to their own husbands, so that the word of God will not be discredited.
In the same way, urge the younger men to be self-controlled.
In everything, show yourself to be an example by doing good works. In your teaching show integrity, dignity,
and wholesome speech that is above reproach, so that anyone who opposes us will be ashamed, having nothing bad to say about us.
Slaves are to submit to their own masters in everything, to be well-pleasing, not argumentative,
not stealing from them, but showing all good faith, so that in every respect they will adorn the teaching about God our {{soter|Savior}}.

units.label: The grace of God has appeared
units.note: the gospel that trains and redeems

verses.text: For the {{charis|grace}} of God has {{epiphaneia|appeared}}, bringing salvation to everyone.
It instructs us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live sensible, upright, and godly lives in the present age,
as we await the blessed hope and glorious {{epiphaneia|appearance}} of our {{megasTheos|great God and Savior}} Jesus Christ.
He gave Himself for us to {{lutroo|redeem}} us from all lawlessness and to purify for Himself a people for His own possession, zealous for good deeds.
Speak these things as you encourage and rebuke with all authority. Let no one despise you.

hygiaino.gloss: To be healthy or sound, the root of hygiene. Applied to doctrine, it means teaching that is spiritually wholesome and life-giving, the opposite of the diseased talk of the false teachers. Sound doctrine in Titus is never abstract: it produces the healthy, godly lives described in this very chapter.

sophron.gloss: Self-controlled, sensible, of a sound mind. The word and its cognates saturate this chapter, urged on older men, young women, and young men, and named as the very thing grace trains us toward in verse 12. It is the mark of a people no longer ruled by worldly passions but governed by God's grace.

soter.gloss: Saviour, one who rescues. A key title in Titus, applied to both God the Father and Christ Jesus. Here even the humble faithfulness of slaves is said to adorn the teaching about God our Saviour, showing that the gospel's beauty is displayed in ordinary, honest lives.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's undeserved favour and saving kindness. In verse 11 grace is almost personified: it has appeared in the person and work of Christ, bringing salvation and actively training God's people. Grace is not only the ground of pardon but the power that reshapes how we live.

epiphaneia.gloss: An appearing or manifestation, used of a royal or divine arrival. Titus 2 speaks of two such appearings: grace has already appeared in Christ's first coming, verse 11, and we await His glorious appearing at the second, verse 13. Christian life is lived between these two shining moments.

megasTheos.gloss: Our great God and Savior. By a well-known feature of Greek grammar, the single phrase most naturally refers to one person, Jesus Christ, making this one of the clearest New Testament statements of Christ's full deity. The one we await is our great God and our Saviour in one.

lutroo.gloss: To redeem, to ransom by paying a price to set free. It recalls Israel's redemption from Egypt and the price of a slave's release. Christ gave Himself as the ransom to free us from all lawlessness and to make us His own purified people, so redemption leads straight to a transformed, good-works life.

basic.context: In contrast to the false teachers, Titus is to teach truth that produces good lives. Paul goes group by group, older men and women, younger women and men, and slaves, describing the godly character each should show. Then he explains the power behind it all: the grace of God that appeared in Jesus to save us, train us, and make us His own people, eager to do good.
basic.hermeneutics: The instructions to different groups reflect the household structures of the first century, and the underlying call, to godliness, self-control, and integrity that make the gospel attractive, holds for every age. The section on slaves describes how to live faithfully within an existing system; it is not an endorsement of slavery. The gospel statement in verses 11-14 is the key that unlocks the whole passage.
basic.originalLanguages: Titus is to speak what accords with hygiaino, sound or healthy, doctrine. Self-control, sophron, runs through the chapter. Everything aims to adorn the teaching about God our soter, our Saviour. The heart is the charis, grace, that has appeared, epiphaneia, in Christ, our great God and Saviour, who gave Himself to redeem, lutroo, a people for Himself.
basic.typology: Christ giving Himself to redeem a people for His own possession echoes the exodus, when God ransomed Israel to be His treasured possession. Jesus is the greater Redeemer, and the church is the true treasured people, purified by His blood.
basic.christ: This is one of the clearest gospel summaries in the New Testament. The grace of God has appeared in Jesus at His first coming; He gave Himself to redeem us and purify a people zealous for good works; and we now wait for His glorious second appearing. Verse 13 calls Him our great God and Saviour, a plain statement of His full deity.
basic.god: God is our Saviour whose grace has appeared to everyone. His grace does not merely forgive; it teaches and trains us to say no to ungodliness and to live upright lives. He is a God who redeems a people to belong to Him and to be eager for good.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2 shows salvation by grace leading to good works God prepared. First Peter 2 calls the church a people for God's own possession. And Exodus 19 names Israel God's treasured possession, the pattern fulfilled in Christ's redeemed people.
basic.oneStory: The gospel here spans the whole story: grace appearing in Christ's first coming, a people redeemed and purified as God's own possession like Israel of old, and the sure hope of His glorious return. We live in the middle, between the two appearings, being trained by grace to live godly lives that make the gospel beautiful.

context.literary: A classic household code, addressing groups in turn, framed by charges to Titus in verses 1 and 15, and anchored by the great doctrinal statement of verses 11-14. The word for, at the start of verse 11, shows the gospel is the ground and motive of all the preceding instructions.
context.historical: The Greco-Roman household, with its ordered relationships including slavery, was the basic unit of society. A new faith could be dismissed as socially disruptive, so Paul stresses conduct that gives critics nothing to attack and makes the gospel appealing in a watching, often hostile culture.
context.canonical: Alongside Ephesians 5-6, Colossians 3-4, and 1 Peter 2-3, this is one of the New Testament household passages. Its gospel core, the two appearings and the self-giving of Christ, stands among the richest concise statements of salvation in Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To equip Titus to teach a godliness that fits sound doctrine across the whole church, and to root that entire way of life in the saving, training grace of God revealed in Christ, so that belief and behaviour adorn the gospel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The call to godliness, self-control, purity, and integrity is prescriptive for all believers. The specific household roles reflect the first-century setting; the instructions to slaves describe faithful living within a system Paul does not thereby endorse, as the wider New Testament trajectory makes clear.

debates.question: Does grace bringing salvation to everyone teach universal salvation?

views.label: Grace is offered to all kinds of people
views.case: The point is that grace has appeared for people of every station, the groups just listed, not that every individual is finally saved; salvation comes through faith in Christ.

views.label: Grace is universally available, not automatically applied
views.case: Grace has appeared to all, offering salvation to the whole world, yet it saves those who receive it, and Paul immediately speaks of a particular people purified for God.

debates.bearing: Either way, the verse magnifies the reach of grace without teaching that all are saved apart from Christ; the passage speaks of a redeemed, purified people.

debates.question: Who is the great God and Savior Jesus Christ in verse 13?

views.label: One person, Jesus, called both God and Savior
views.case: Greek grammar (the Granville Sharp construction) most naturally makes the single phrase refer to Christ alone, a direct affirmation of His deity.

views.label: Two persons, God and Christ
views.case: A minority read it as the Father, our great God, and the Son, our Savior; but the grammar and the focus on Christ's appearing favour the single-person reading.

debates.bearing: The stronger reading confesses Jesus as our great God and Saviour, one of the New Testament's clearest windows onto His divine identity.

study.originalLanguages: Titus is to speak what fits hygiaino doctrine, teaching that is spiritually healthy rather than the diseased talk of the deceivers, and the health shows in sophron, self-control, a virtue that recurs across every group and reappears as grace's own aim in verse 12. Even slaves, by honest faithfulness, adorn the teaching about God our soter. The theological heart turns on charis, grace personified, that has made its epiphaneia, its shining appearance, in Christ's first coming, with a second epiphaneia awaited in glory. Verse 13 names Jesus our megas theos kai soter, our great God and Savior, most naturally one person by the grammar, and verse 14 says He gave Himself to lutroo, to ransom, a people for His own possession, echoing the exodus redemption.

typology.type: Christ redeeming a people for His own possession
typology.fulfillment: Fulfils the exodus, where God ransomed Israel from Egypt to be His treasured possession, Exodus 19:5, now realised in the church purified by Christ's blood.
Christ's first coming in humility to save and His glorious second coming in power, framing the whole age of the church.

study.christ: Verses 11-14 are a compact gospel. The grace of God appeared in the person of Jesus at His first coming, bringing salvation and training His people in godliness. He gave Himself, a willing self-sacrifice, to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify a people who are His own, eager to do good. And we await His blessed hope, the glorious appearing of our great God and Saviour Jesus Christ, verse 13 confessing His full deity. Christ is thus the content of grace, the price of redemption, and the returning King for whom we watch.
study.god: God is our Saviour whose grace has appeared to people of every kind. That grace is not indulgent; it actively instructs us to renounce ungodliness and to live sensible, upright, godly lives. God gives His own Son to ransom a people and to make them His treasured possession, zealous for good deeds. He is a God who both saves freely and forms a holy people who belong to Him.

crossReferences.note: Saved by grace, not works, yet created for good works God prepared, the same grace-then-good-works pattern.
A people for God's own possession, called to declare His praises, echoing verse 14.
Israel as God's treasured possession, the exodus pattern Christ fulfils in redeeming the church.
Christ gave Himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age, parallel to He gave Himself for us.

study.oneStory: This passage stretches across the whole biblical story: grace appearing in Christ's first coming, a people ransomed and purified as God's own possession, just as Israel was redeemed from Egypt, and the certain hope of Christ's glorious return. We live between the two appearings, in the present age, being trained by grace to live lives that make the gospel beautiful, until our great God and Saviour appears.

sermon.title: The Grace of God Appeared
sermon.why: In this Look at the Book study on Titus 2:11-14, Piper traces how the grace of God appeared in the person of Jesus, not a mere message, and how that grace both saves and trains us as we await the blessed hope. A clear, orthodox exposition of this movement's gospel heart.

quiz.q: What is the aim of the godly conduct Paul urges on each group?

options.text: That the word of God and the teaching about our Savior will be adorned, not discredited.
options.note: Verses 5 and 10 tie behaviour to the credibility and beauty of the gospel.

options.text: That believers will earn their salvation by good behaviour.
options.note: Good conduct flows from grace, verse 11; it does not earn salvation.

options.text: That Christians will win social status and comfort.
options.note: The goal is the gospel's honour, not personal advancement.

options.text: That the church will withdraw from the watching world.
options.note: The point is a witness before outsiders, not withdrawal.

quiz.q: According to verse 11, what has the grace of God done?

options.text: It has appeared in Christ, bringing salvation to everyone.
options.note: Grace has appeared, the gospel's first appearing, bringing salvation.

options.text: It has appeared only to a spiritual elite.
options.note: Grace has appeared for people of every station, not a secret few.

options.text: It has removed all need for godly living.
options.note: The very next verse says grace trains us to live godly lives.

options.text: It has not yet appeared and remains only future.
options.note: Grace has already appeared in Christ's first coming; we await the second.

quiz.q: What does grace train us to do while we wait for Christ?

options.text: To renounce ungodliness and worldly passions and live self-controlled, upright, godly lives.
options.note: Verse 12 makes grace an active teacher of holy living.

options.text: To do whatever we please, since we are forgiven.
options.note: That misreads grace; grace instructs us to renounce ungodliness.

options.text: To withdraw and simply wait passively.
options.note: We wait actively, living godly lives in the present age.

options.text: To trust in our own good works for acceptance.
options.note: Grace, not works, saves; it then reshapes how we live.

quiz.q: Who is described as our great God and Savior in verse 13?

options.text: Jesus Christ, named as both our great God and our Savior.
options.note: The grammar most naturally applies the whole phrase to Christ, affirming His deity.

options.text: Only God the Father, separate from Jesus.
options.note: A minority read it this way, but the phrase points to Christ Himself.

options.text: An angel sent to represent God.
options.note: The text speaks of God and Savior, not an angel.

options.text: Titus, as God's appointed leader.
options.note: The title belongs to Christ, whose appearing we await.

quiz.q: Why did Christ give Himself for us, according to verse 14?

options.text: To redeem us from all lawlessness and purify a people for His own, zealous for good deeds.
options.note: Redemption produces a purified people eager to do good, verse 14.

options.text: To leave us exactly as we were.
options.note: He redeems in order to purify and transform, not to leave us unchanged.

options.text: To reward those who first made themselves worthy.
options.note: He gave Himself for us; the initiative and price are His, not ours.

options.text: To free us so we can live however we like.
options.note: He frees us from lawlessness to make us zealous for good deeds.

quiz.q: How should Titus deliver this teaching, according to verse 15?

options.text: Speaking, encouraging, and rebuking with all authority, letting no one despise him.
options.note: Verse 15 charges Titus to teach boldly and with authority.

options.text: Timidly, so as not to upset anyone.
options.note: He is to encourage and rebuke with authority, not shrink back.

options.text: Only in private, never openly.
options.note: He is to speak these things openly and with authority.

options.text: As his personal opinion, carrying no weight.
options.note: He speaks with all authority as Christ's minister.

questions: Paul ties everyday conduct to whether the gospel looks beautiful or is discredited. Where might your daily behaviour be commending or undermining the message you profess?
Grace does not only forgive; it trains us to say no to ungodliness. What is one worldly passion grace is teaching you to renounce right now?
We live between Christ's first appearing and His glorious return. How would living with the blessed hope in view change your priorities this week?
Christ gave Himself to make us a people zealous for good deeds. Does zeal for doing good mark your life, and where is God stirring you toward it?
Older believers here are called to train the younger by example and instruction. Who is pouring godly wisdom into you, and into whom could you be pouring it?

translationNotes.note: Bringing salvation to everyone can be read as grace appearing to all people, offering salvation, rather than teaching that every individual is finally saved; the context speaks of a purified people.
The blessed hope and glorious appearance of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ most naturally, by the Greek construction, calls Jesus both God and Saviour, affirming His deity.
A people for His own possession echoes the Greek term for a treasured possession, recalling Israel in Exodus 19:5 and Deuteronomy 14:2.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Titus 2:1-15. No em or en dashes appear in this passage.
